items.q: How do you write 'Christmas' and 'Easter' in Spanish?
items.correct: Navidad y Pascua (note: Semana Santa for Holy Week traditions).
items.why: Students add unnecessary accents or use English. Navidad has NO accent (stress on the final syllable but ends in -d which is naturally stressed). Pascua follows normal stress rules. Customs vocab: Reyes Magos (Three Kings), feria (fair).

items.q: How do you say 'My sister is embarrassed' in Spanish?
items.correct: Mi hermana tiene vergüenza / está avergonzada.
items.why: Notorious mistake when discussing family/relationships. 'Embarazada' = PREGNANT, not embarrassed. To say embarrassed: tener vergüenza or estar avergonzado/a. Saying your sister is pregnant when you mean embarrassed causes laughter.

items.q: Which preposition expresses purpose ('in order to')?
items.why: Students treat por and para as identical 'for'. PARA = destination, purpose, recipient, deadline. POR = reason/cause, exchange, duration, motion through. The distinction is regularly tested.

items.q: Which is the imperfect of 'estudiar' for 'él'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: estudiaba. “estudia” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the university entrance exam called?
items.why: It's EvAU. “ESO” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Up to what age is compulsory schooling in Spain (end of ESO)?
items.why: The real figure is 16 years. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
